title: Quick check: scheduled-task, service, and file remediations

questions.prompt: You approve a scheduled-task remediation while the task is currently running. What happens?
choices: The remediation removes the task entry. The currently-running instance finishes its current iteration; the task doesn't re-fire.

choices.label: The remediation kills the running process and removes the task entry simultaneously.
choices.rationale: The remediation doesn't kill the running instance. That would need a separate process-kill or isolation.

choices.label: The remediation fails because the task is in use.
choices.rationale: The task entry is removed regardless of whether an iteration is currently running.

choices.label: The remediation waits for the next reboot before applying.
choices.rationale: The remediation applies at the agent's next check-in, not at reboot.

questions.explanation: Bounded blast radius. Entry removed; running instance finishes; no re-fire.

questions.prompt: The "new signal" case (customer's IT contact recognising the affected object as legitimate) shows up MORE often with which remediation type, and why?
choices: Services — because services tend to be deliberately set up by IT, so someone in the customer's organisation knew about it. Verify before approving in environments rich with custom tooling.

choices.label: Scheduled tasks — every task is set up by a customer admin.
choices.rationale: Scheduled tasks more often have a documented business purpose someone forgot to flag, but services are the more frequent new-signal case because they're more deliberate.

choices.label: Files — customers always recognise their own files.
choices.rationale: LOB-file misclassification is less common than for services; the SOC's classification on files is usually careful.

choices.label: Autoruns — the registry surface is the most-customised on Windows.
choices.rationale: Services are the higher-rate new-signal case.

questions.explanation: Services are deliberately installed by IT more often than scheduled tasks; the customer-recognises-it case is more frequent.

questions.prompt: A tech approves a service remediation with the same speed and inattention as an autoruns remediation, and is surprised when it takes longer or breaks something. What's the failure?
choices: Treating all three remediation types as interchangeable. The blast-radius spectrum is the thing to carry.

choices.label: Failing to read the Recommendation.
choices.rationale: The tech did read it; the failure is treating the types as interchangeable.

choices.label: Skipping the claim step.
choices.rationale: Different failure mode.

choices.label: Calling the customer before reading the Evidence.
choices.rationale: Not the named failure here.

questions.explanation: Each remediation type has a different blast radius. Same workflow shape; different diligence on review and verify.

questions.prompt: The Recommendation includes a service remediation on a server, but the customer hasn't confirmed it's safe to stop the service. What's the right move?
choices: Bump rather than approve blind. The "verify with user" pairing matters more on services on servers than on most workstations.

choices.label: Approve immediately; the SOC's Recommendation overrides server-impact concerns.
choices.rationale: The SOC usually accounts for server impact; when customer confirmation is part of the pattern and you can't reach them, bumping is the call.

choices.label: Wait until after-hours to approve.
choices.rationale: No after-hours rule; customer confirmation or bump is what the lesson asks for.

choices.label: Approve the service remediation but skip the file removal.
choices.rationale: Softening the recommendation, which is a separate failure.

questions.explanation: Server-side service has the largest blast radius. Confirm or bump; don't approve blind.

questions.prompt: Low EDR Incident Report. Host: SRV-EXAMPLE-DB-01. Recommendation: approve the service remediation. Evidence shows a service named HelperUpdater with elevated privileges, binary at C:\ProgramData\HU\hu.exe. Customer's IT manager is unreachable. What's the right move?

choices.label: Approve the service remediation, document, close. The SOC has triaged.
choices.rationale: Service remediation on a server has the largest blast radius; the five-second sanity check is worth doing even with SOC triage.

choices: Hold the incident open with a note, reach the IT manager via documented backup contact, then approve once confirmed unknown / not legitimate.

choices.label: Escalate to a senior immediately.
choices.rationale: Premature. The Recommendation is in the standard helpdesk workflow; you just need user confirmation before clicking.

questions.explanation: Service on server + unfamiliar service name + customer unreachable = pause-and-confirm via the backup contact.

questions.prompt: Continuing the SRV-EXAMPLE-DB-01 scenario: the IT manager confirms HelperUpdater is not legitimate. You approve, the portal shows completed in 90 seconds, the service is gone and the binary is in quarantine. Within 45 minutes, a new incident: same service name re-appearing on the same host. What do you do?

choices.label: Approve the remediation again; it worked the first time.
choices.rationale: Same pattern as autoruns Decision 2: the remediation removed it, something on the endpoint re-installed it. Re-approving is whack-a-mole.

choices: Recognise the pattern (clean removal, re-establishment within an hour), isolate the host, escalate to senior in parallel.

choices.label: Re-approve the service remediation and approve any new remediation that lands in the next hour.
choices.rationale: Worst response. Playing whack-a-mole on persistence being actively re-established.

questions.explanation: Same containment-and-escalate trigger as autoruns re-detection. The remediation type doesn't matter; the pattern does.

questions.prompt: The lesson recommends a quick check before approving a file remediation. What is it?
choices: Does the affected file path look like part of a customer's line-of-business application? Five-second sanity check; if it looks LOB-shaped, bump.

choices.label: Has Huntress support approved the file remediation in writing?
choices.rationale: Misuses support and isn't the lesson's check.

choices.label: Has the file hash been checked against VirusTotal?
choices.rationale: Investigation above the helpdesk ceiling; the SOC has classified.

choices.label: Have you rebooted the endpoint first?
choices.rationale: Reboot isn't part of the remediation flow and isn't a pre-check.

questions.explanation: LOB-shaped file path → bump. Five-second check, cheap to do, catches the misclassified-DLL case.

questions.prompt: Across the three remediation types, where should diligence on review and verify scale MOST sharply?
choices: With the blast radius. Scheduled tasks are easiest to approve confidently; services need user verification more often; files need a sanity check for LOB shape.

choices.label: With the severity tag. High needs more diligence; Low needs less.
choices.rationale: Severity drives tempo; blast radius drives diligence. A server-side Low can have a larger blast radius than a workstation High.

choices.label: With the customer's MSP tier.
choices.rationale: Tier isn't part of the framework. Diligence isn't gated by commercial tier.

choices.label: Equally across all three; the workflow is identical.
choices.rationale: Workflow is the same shape, but diligence scales with blast radius.

questions.explanation: Same workflow, scaling diligence. The blast radius is what scales it.

questions.prompt: How long should you watch for re-detection after a clean service or file remediation?
choices: About an hour. A re-detection in that window is a containment-and-escalate signal regardless of remediation type.

choices.label: Five minutes is enough; the portal confirms applied quickly.
choices.rationale: Too short. The hour window catches the persistence-had-a-friend pattern.

choices.label: 24 hours; effects propagate slowly.
choices.rationale: Too long for active monitoring. The lesson uses the hour window for escalation triggering.

choices.label: Until the customer's next business day.
choices.rationale: Calendar-driven, not signal-driven.

questions.explanation: One hour. The persistence-had-a-friend pattern surfaces in that window.

questions.prompt: A service remediation has a larger blast radius than a scheduled-task remediation because stopping a service halts a running process, while removing a scheduled task only stops future iterations.
questions.explanation: The lesson's framing exactly. Services stop a live process; scheduled tasks let the running instance finish before stopping.
